subject: Update db-migrator to include rollback functionality
commit_message:
Update db-migrator to include rollback functionality

Issue-ID: POLICY-3490
Change-Id: Ibd1898819a5cb70f25bf69d71f2975dd2845389e
Signed-off-by: ktimoney <kevin.timoney@est.tech>

entries.message: Uploaded patch set 1.

Patch Set 1:

Build Started https://jenkins.onap.org/job/policy-docker-master-verify-java/260/

Patch Set 1: Verified+1

Build Successful 

https://jenkins.onap.org/job/policy-docker-master-verify-java/260/ : SUCCESS

Logs: https://logs.onap.org/production/vex-yul-ecomp-jenkins-1/policy-docker-master-verify-java/260

Patch Set 1:

if an upgrade fails in the middle, how does this rollback start from the last successful script?  And how does it invoke the rollback/downgrade scripts in the reverse order?  (Similar question for downgrading.)

Patch Set 1:

> Patch Set 1:
> 
> if an upgrade fails in the middle, how does this rollback start from the last successful script?  And how does it invoke the rollback/downgrade scripts in the reverse order?  (Similar question for downgrading.)

The upgrade function includes this block of code:
                if [ ${rc} -ne 0 ]; then
                    echo "${SCHEMA}: upgrade aborted at ${schemaVersion} by script ${sqlName}"
                    set_current_release "${schemaVersion}"
                    rollback "${PREVIOUS_RELEASE}" "${schemaVersion}" "${tag}"
                    return ${rc}
                fi
so if a script fails in the middle of an upgrade the rollback is triggered:
rollback "${PREVIOUS_RELEASE}" "${schemaVersion}" "${tag}"

this calls the rollback function which retrieves the list of scripts that were run successfully during the upgrade.
The history tables has a couple of new columns, id and tag.
id is a sequence number and tag groups upgrade/downgrade operations together.
tags are made up from date/time + version + single letter operation code (u/d/r)
The records (script names) are retrieved using the tag and are returned in reverse id order.
The names of the upgrade and rollback (downgrade scripts) are the same so it just runs the rollback versions. There is no rollback for downgrade at the moment.

so if there are 100 upgrade scripts and it fails at script 50, it will begin rolling back by executing downgrade script 49, 48, etc.?  I didn't notice that the script name was being stored, so wasn't sure how it knew which one to execute.  Guess I should look more closely - maybe it just stores the script's sequence number (as the "id").

> > The history tables has a couple of new columns, id and tag.
> > id is a sequence number and tag groups upgrade/downgrade operations together.
> > tags are made up from date/time + version + single letter operation code (u/d/r)
> > The records (script names) are retrieved using the tag and are returned in reverse id order.
> > The names of the upgrade and rollback (downgrade scripts) are the same so it just runs the rollback versions. There is no rollback for downgrade at the moment.
> 
> so if there are 100 upgrade scripts and it fails at script 50, it will begin rolling back by executing downgrade script 49, 48, etc.?  I didn't notice that the script name was being stored, so wasn't sure how it knew which one to execute.  Guess I should look more closely - maybe it just stores the script's sequence number (as the "id").

Yes, that's right. If you look at the end of the sample output on the WIKI https://wiki.onap.org/display/DW/Instructions+for+running+db-migrator you'll get a better idea of what's going on.
The id is a separate sequence number to track all operations and in what order they occurred.
The name of the script is stored in the script field e.g. 0100-jpapolicyaudit.sql.
Tags take the format of 1907210941060900u - these are unique to each operation/version (0900u).
So when you hit an operation that's not successful all you need to do is query for the tag and order by id desc. An error in a 0900 version upgrade will only rollback 0900 version files, 0800 will be left alone.

Patch Set 1:

> Yes, that's right. If you look at the end of the sample output on the WIKI https://wiki.onap.org/display/DW/Instructions+for+running+db-migrator you'll get a better idea of what's going on.
> The id is a separate sequence number to track all operations and in what order they occurred.
> The name of the script is stored in the script field e.g. 0100-jpapolicyaudit.sql.
> Tags take the format of 1907210941060900u - these are unique to each operation/version (0900u).
> So when you hit an operation that's not successful all you need to do is query for the tag and order by id desc. An error in a 0900 version upgrade will only rollback 0900 version files, 0800 will be left alone.

Cool, thanks!  So if there are three upgrade/downgrades (or upgrade/rollbacks), then the next time it downgrades (or rolls back), it will only do the downgrade associated with the last upgrade (i.e., the records from the previous runs will not interfere with the latest attempt)?

Patch Set 1:

> Patch Set 1:
> 
> > Yes, that's right. If you look at the end of the sample output on the WIKI https://wiki.onap.org/display/DW/Instructions+for+running+db-migrator you'll get a better idea of what's going on.
> > The id is a separate sequence number to track all operations and in what order they occurred.
> > The name of the script is stored in the script field e.g. 0100-jpapolicyaudit.sql.
> > Tags take the format of 1907210941060900u - these are unique to each operation/version (0900u).
> > So when you hit an operation that's not successful all you need to do is query for the tag and order by id desc. An error in a 0900 version upgrade will only rollback 0900 version files, 0800 will be left alone.
> 
> Cool, thanks!  So if there are three upgrade/downgrades (or upgrade/rollbacks), then the next time it downgrades (or rolls back), it will only do the downgrade associated with the last upgrade (i.e., the records from the previous runs will not interfere with the latest attempt)?

Yes that's correct. That's the reason I included the tag field so we are careful to only rollback exactly what we want. There may be multiple upgrades and downgrades to a particular schema and we want to track them all but also be able to differentiate between them.

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

> Patch Set 1:
> 
> > Patch Set 1:
> > 
> > if an upgrade fails in the middle, how does this rollback start from the last successful script?  And how does it invoke the rollback/downgrade scripts in the reverse order?  (Similar question for downgrading.)

> The upgrade function includes this block of code:

> this calls the rollback function which retrieves the list of scripts that were run successfully during the upgrade.
> The history tables has a couple of new columns, id and tag.
> id is a sequence number and tag groups upgrade/downgrade operations together.
> tags are made up from date/time + version + single letter operation code (u/d/r)
> The records (script names) are retrieved using the tag and are returned in reverse id order.
> The names of the upgrade and rollback (downgrade scripts) are the same so it just runs the rollback versions. There is no rollback for downgrade at the moment.

I don't think rollback should silently fallback to an earlier version (which will most likely won't play nicely if succeeds with the rest of the components being upgraded, it should be all or nothing for all components, ie. api, etc ..).  If the upgrade fails typically it will require manual intervention as there could be db corruption or other unanticipated problem, in any case, I think the operations folks should know.  I thought the tool already has options to do these operations manually.  In any case, I suggest to not deal with automated rollback at this point in time, and do it when the framework for migrating data is proven solid, but we should have mechanisms in the tool to give ops people the means to fix it (I thought there were already options to do it) and we could revisit rollback automation at a future point.

Patch Set 1:

> I don't think rollback should silently fallback to an earlier version (which will most likely won't play nicely if succeeds with the rest of the components being upgraded, it should be all or nothing for all components, ie. api, etc ..).  If the upgrade fails typically it will require manual intervention as there could be db corruption or other unanticipated problem, in any case, I think the operations folks should know.  I thought the tool already has options to do these operations manually.  In any case, I suggest to not deal with automated rollback at this point in time, and do it when the framework for migrating data is proven solid, but we should have mechanisms in the tool to give ops people the means to fix it (I thought there were already options to do it) and we could revisit rollback automation at a future point.

I don't think we were planning to roll back automatically.  However, the idea was to record exactly where it failed, so the downgrade scripts could be executed to perform a rollback from where it broke, or the user could intervene to fix something manually and then continue the upgrade from where it left off.

Yes the report will show the success field as 0 (second last one) if a script fails.
| 103 | 0100-jpapolicyaudit.sql | upgrade | 0800 | 0900 | 1907211410140900u | 1 | 2021-07-19 14:10:14 |
| 104 | 0110-sequence.sql | upgrade | 0800 | 0900 | 1907211410140900u | 1 | 2021-07-19 14:10:14 |
| 105 | 0120-JpaPolicyAuditIndex_timestamp.sql | upgrade | 0800 | 0900 | 1907211410140900u | 0 | 2021-07-19 14:10:14 |
In this case 0120-JpaPolicyAuditIndex_timestamp.sql has failed.
rollback is slightly different to downgrade, rollback may not include all the downgrade scripts.
Trying to downgrade an incomplete upgrade will also fail.
But if you are doing it manually you can remove any scripts from the downgrade directory that are not required.

> rollback is slightly different to downgrade, rollback may not include all the downgrade scripts.
> Trying to downgrade an incomplete upgrade will also fail.
> But if you are doing it manually you can remove any scripts from the downgrade directory that are not required.

Not sure why rollback and downgrade should be any different: just look at what's in the DB and start executing in reverse order until you reach the required version.  One caveat is that the most recently executed script should be skipped or downgraded, depending on whether or not its flag indicated failure or success, respectively
